---
title: "USDCx and the freeze list"
subtitle: "Compliance without surveillance, in production."
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status:** Stub. Full chapter coming soon. (For the cryptographic primitive
that powers this chapter, see [Merkle trees](../02-cryptographic-primitives/02-merkle-trees.qmd) — that's the
foundation; this chapter shows how Circle and Paxos actually deploy it.)
:::

## The question

Circle issues USDCx (`usdcx_stablecoin.aleo`) on Aleo mainnet. Paxos issues
USAD (`usad_stablecoin.aleo`). Both are subject to OFAC sanctions. Both
need to block sanctioned addresses while preserving privacy for everyone else.

How do they do it?

## What this chapter will cover

- The 3-program pattern: `*_stablecoin.aleo` + `*_freezelist.aleo` + `*_multisig_core.aleo`
- The freeze list as a sorted Merkle tree (revisits the
  [Merkle chapter](../02-cryptographic-primitives/02-merkle-trees.qmd) with real bytecode)
- All 8 transfer variants in USDCx, by privacy pair: public/private × from/to
  - `transfer_public` (no Merkle proof needed)
  - `transfer_public_to_private` (no Merkle proof — entry point for newcomers)
  - `transfer_private` (requires `[MerkleProof; 2]` for sender + recipient)
  - `transfer_private_to_public` (requires `[MerkleProof; 2]`)
  - `transfer_from_public` and `_public_to_private` (allowance-based)
  - `transfer_private_with_creds` (uses Credentials record)
- The compliance API contract: what Circle's proof server returns, in what format
- The `Credentials` record pattern (precomputed compliance proof attached to a record)
- The pause flag: how the multisig can freeze the entire token in an emergency
- Mainnet vs testnet program IDs: `usdcx_stablecoin.aleo` vs `test_usdcx_stablecoin.aleo`

## Interactive viz planned

A "transfer wizard": pick a privacy pair (public/public, public/private, etc.)
and a token (USDCx, USAD), watch the wizard show:
- What inputs are needed
- What records are consumed/produced
- What public state changes
- What proof obligations are enforced

## Verified mainnet endpoints used in this chapter

```bash
curl https://api.explorer.provable.com/v1/mainnet/program/usdcx_stablecoin.aleo
curl https://api.explorer.provable.com/v1/mainnet/program/usdcx_freezelist.aleo
curl https://api.explorer.provable.com/v1/mainnet/program/usdcx_multisig_core.aleo
curl https://api.explorer.provable.com/v1/mainnet/program/usad_stablecoin.aleo
curl https://api.explorer.provable.com/v1/mainnet/program/usad_freezelist.aleo
curl https://api.explorer.provable.com/v1/mainnet/program/usad_multisig_core.aleo
```

All six were live (HTTP 200) at the time of this book's last verification.

## Why this matters

USDCx and USAD are the **first major regulated stablecoins on a privacy chain**.
Their architecture is a template for any future compliance-heavy privacy
app — KYC pools, accredited-investor flows, sanctions-screened DeFi.
Read it once, and you can recognize the same pattern in any future deployment.
